metastasectomy with complete resection of disease, within a year of 
nephrectomy. Other recommendations for stage IV disease are covered 
later in this Discussion.
Due to the lack of evidence on the role of adjuvant pembrolizumab therapy 
for patients with RCC with non-clear cell histology (nccRCC), the Panel 
does not recommend including it as a treatment option for non-clear cell 
histology. Instead, surveillance is recommended following primary 
treatment for stage II, III, and resectable T4,M0 nccRCC. For patients with 
stage III or resectable T4,M0 nccRCC, clinical trial is also an option after 
primary treatment.

header: Follow-up After Treatment of Localized Disease
content:
After surgical excision, 20% to 30% of patients with localized tumors 
experience relapse. Lung metastasis is the most common site of distant 
recurrence, occurring in 50% to 60% of patients.104 The median time to 
relapse after surgery is 1 to 2 years, with most relapses occurring within 3 
years.105
The Panel has provided a framework for follow-up of patients undergoing 
surveillance of a small renal mass and for patients who underwent surgery 
or ablative therapy for primary RCC. The Panel has reiterated in a footnote 
that no single follow-up plan is appropriate for everyone, and follow-up 
should be modified for the individual patient using clinical judgment. Since 
uniform consensus among the Panel members regarding the most 
appropriate follow-up plan is lacking, these recommendations are listed as 
category 2B. Also, the guidance for follow-up has been provided for the 
first 5 years after nephrectomy, with follow-up evaluation to be extended 
beyond 5 years at the discretion of the physician. Results from a 
retrospective analysis indicate that in a subset of patients, relapses occur 
more than 5 years after surgery for their primary RCC.106 The analysis 
suggests that continued follow-up/surveillance after 5 years may be of
potential value in some patients. Another retrospective analysis suggests 
that patients with lower risk are more likely to relapse later.107 Identification 
of subsets of patients with higher risk who require longer follow-up has not 
been defined, and further research is required to refine follow-up 
strategies for patients with RCC.
The Oncology Guidelines incorporate a risk-stratified use of imaging that may 
target those patients most in need of intensive surveillance and/or imaging 
tests during follow-up.

header: Follow-up During Active Surveillance for Stage I
content:
For follow-up during active surveillance, the Panel recommends an annual 
history and physical (H&P) examination and annual laboratory tests as 
clinically indicated. In order to study the growth rate of the tumor, the 
Panel recommends abdomen imaging (CT or MRI both with and without IV 
contrast, unless otherwise contraindicated) within 6 months from initiation 
of active surveillance; subsequent imaging (with CT, MRI, or ultrasound 
[US]) may be performed annually thereafter. All three modalities (US, CT, 
and MRI) have been found to accurately predict pathologic tumor size in a 
retrospective analysis.108 Therefore, best clinical judgment should be used 
in choosing the imaging modality. The Panel recommends chest x-ray or 
chest CT at baseline and annually as clinically indicated to assess 
pulmonary metastases. Repeat chest imaging can be considered if 
intervention is being contemplated. The Panel notes that follow-up may be 
individualized based on surgical status, treatment schedules, side effects, 
comorbidities, and symptoms.

header: Follow-up After Percutaneous Ablation for Stage I
content:
Most follow-up tests after percutaneous ablation included by the Panel are 
similar to those recommended during active surveillance. For imaging, the 
Panel recommends abdomen CT and MRI both with and without IV 
contrast (unless otherwise contraindicated), or contrast-enhanced US at 1
PLEASE NOTE that use of this Oncology Guidelines Content is governed by the End-User License Agreement, and you MAY NOT distribute this Content or use it with any artificial intelligence model or tool.

Oncology Guidelines Version 2.2026 
Kidney Cancer
MS-10
to 3 months, 6 months, and 12 months following ablation. Subsequent 
imaging is recommended annually. If the patient cannot receive IV 
contrast, MRI or contrast-enhanced US are preferred. If imaging results or 
clinical findings suggest residual disease or recurrence, then biopsy or 
further treatment may be indicated.
For those who have biopsy-proven low-risk pathologic features (no 
sarcomatoid, low-grade [grade 1/2] RCC), non-diagnostic biopsies, or no 
prior biopsy, the Panel also recommends annual chest CT for 5 years to 
assess for pulmonary metastases.

header: Follow-up After SBRT for Stage I
content:
The Panel also includes follow-up recommendations for imaging and 
assessing renal function for patients who have stage I RCC and received 
SBRT. An abdomen/chest CT is recommended every 3 months in the first 
year, every 6 months in the second year, every 9 months in the third and 
fourth year, and annually in the fifth year. Renal function should be 
assessed using the same schedule as imaging.

header: Follow-up After Partial or Radical Nephrectomy for Stages I–II
content:
For patients with stage I or II RCC who underwent a partial or radical 
nephrectomy, the Panel recommends an annual H&P examination and 
annual laboratory tests as clinically indicated. For patients with stage I 
RCC, the Panel recommends a baseline abdomen CT or MRI (preferred) 
both with and without contrast (unless contraindicated) within 3 to 12 
months following renal surgery, then annually for up to 5 years or longer 
as clinically indicated. For patients with stage II RCC, the Panel 
recommends an increase in abdomen imaging frequency and inclusion of 
pelvis imaging, with baseline abdomen/pelvis CT or MRI (preferred), both 
with and without contrast (unless contraindicated) every 6 months for 2 
years, then annually for up to 5 years or longer as clinically indicated. A 
more rigorous imaging schedule can be considered if the patient has
positive margins or adverse pathologic features (eg, sarcomatoid, grade 
3/4 RCC). The rates of local recurrence for smaller tumors after partial 
nephrectomy are 1.4% to 2% versus 10% for larger tumors.66,109,110 The 
Panel also recommends yearly chest CT for at least 5 years and as 
clinically indicated thereafter. As mentioned above, a more rigorous 
imaging schedule (CT preferred) can be considered if the patient has 
positive margins or adverse pathologic features. Additionally, for any 
patients with stage II RCC who are being considered for adjuvant therapy, 
comprehensive restaging including chest CT should be performed prior to 
initiating treatment.

header: Follow-up for Patients with Stage III or Resected T4,NXM0 RCC
content:
For patients with stage III RCC, larger tumors have a substantially higher 
risk of both local and metastatic recurrence, which warrants an increased 
follow-up frequency compared with patients with stage I or II RCC. 
Therefore, for these patients, the Panel recommends an H&P examination 
every 3 to 6 months for 3 years, then annually for up to 5 years. The 
follow-up evaluation may be extended beyond 5 years at the discretion of 
the physician as clinically indicated. A comprehensive metabolic panel and 
other tests are recommended as indicated every 3 to 6 months for 3 years, 
then annually up to 5 years, and as clinically indicated thereafter.
The Panel recommends baseline abdomen and pelvis CT or MRI (both 
with and without contrast, unless contraindicated) within 3 to 6 months 
following surgery, followed by CT, MRI (preferred), or US every 3 to 6 
months for at least 3 years, and annually thereafter for up to 5 years. 
There is disagreement among the Panel members regarding the 
usefulness of US in patients with stage III disease. Therefore, it is listed as 
a category 2B option specifically for patients with stage III disease.
PLEASE NOTE that use of this Oncology Guidelines Content is governed by the End-User License Agreement, and you MAY NOT distribute this Content or use it with any artificial intelligence model or tool.
Printed by Máximo López Chenlo on 5/4/2026 6:22:18 PM. Copyright © 2026 Oncology Guidelines, Inc. All Rights Reserved.
Version 2.2026 © 2026 Oncology Guidelines© (Oncology Guidelines©), All rights reserved. Oncology Guidelines® and this illustration may not be reproduced in any form without the express written permission of Oncology Guidelines.
Oncology Guidelines Version 2.2026 
Kidney Cancer
MS-11
The Panel also recommends baseline chest CT within 3 to 6 months 
following surgery, followed by continued imaging (CT preferred) every 3 to 
6 months for at least 3 years, and annually thereafter for up to 5 years.
While the use of US imaging for follow-up is an option for patients with 
low-risk RCC, CT or MRI is the preferred modality for those with a high 
risk of recurrence. The Panel notes that imaging beyond 5 years may be 
performed as clinically indicated, and additional site-specific imaging (eg, 
bone scan, brain imaging) may be performed as symptoms warrant.
FDG-PET is also recommended in certain circumstances. One 
retrospective study that identified imaging features for FH-deficient RCC in 
16 patients showed elevated metabolic activity in the tumors imaged using 
FDG-PET/CT (n = 3).38 The Panel recommends FDG-PET be used during 
follow-up if a patient has FH-deficient RCC, and also recommends this 
imaging modality for SDHB-deficient RCC.
For patients who are being considered for adjuvant therapy, restaging 
should be done prior to initiating treatment. Notably, the Panel 
recommends that all patients who receive adjuvant therapy also be given 
follow-up before, during, and after therapy based on the recommendations 
for stage III disease.
Prognostic Tools for Localized Disease
Alternate surveillance programs have been proposed, such as risk-based 
calculators and nomograms that can give 5-year survival estimates. The 
Panel does not explicitly recommend a risk-based calculator for identifying 
high-risk localized disease; however, two examples from the literature for 
identifying patients with higher risk of recurrence include the surveillance 
protocol based on the University of California Los Angeles (UCLA) 
Integrated Staging System (UISS) and the ASSURE RCC prognostic 
nomogram from Fox Chase Cancer Center.111,112 The UISS is an 
evidence-based system in which patients are stratified based on the 1997
TNM (tumor, node, metastasis) stage, grade, and ECOG performance 
status into low-, intermediate-, or high-risk groups for developing 
recurrence or metastases for post-surgical treatment of localized or locally 
advanced RCC.111 The ASSURE RCC prognostic nomogram was created 
for prediction of recurrence in intermediate- and high-risk localized disease 
derived from characteristics of the patient population in the phase III 
ASSURE trial mentioned previously. This model includes histology, tumor 
size and grade, necrosis, and nodal involvement as characteristics, and is 
independent of AJCC TNM staging. It was applied to DFS, OS, and early 
disease progression measures for validation.112
Management of Relapsed or Stage IV (M1 or Unresectable 
T4,M0) Disease
Prognostic Models for Metastatic Disease
Prognostic scoring systems have been developed to define risk groups of 
patients by combining independent prognostic factors for survival in 
patients with metastatic RCC.113,114
The first prognostic factor model to be widely applied was from Memorial 
Sloan Kettering Cancer Center (MSKCC). The model was derived from 
examining prognostic factors in patients (n = 463) with metastatic RCC 
enrolled in clinical trials and treated with interferon (IFN).113 Prognostic 
factors for multivariable analysis included five variables: interval from 
diagnosis to treatment of <1 year; Karnofsky Performance Status (KPS) 
<80%; serum LDH >1.5 times the upper limit of normal (ULN); corrected 
serum calcium greater than the ULN; and serum hemoglobin less than the 
lower limit of normal (LLN). Patients with none of these factors are in the 
favorable risk category with good prognosis, those with one or two factors 
present are considered intermediate risk, and patients with three or more 
of the factors are considered poor risk. The MSKCC criteria have been 
additionally validated by an independent group at the Cleveland Clinic.115

Oncology Guidelines Version 2.2026 
Kidney Cancer
MS-12
A prognostic model derived from a population of patients with metastatic 
RCC treated with vascular endothelial growth factor (VEGF)-targeted 
therapy followed the International Metastatic Renal Cell Carcinoma 
Database Consortium (IMDC) model.114 This model was derived from a 
retrospective study of 645 patients with metastatic RCC treated with 
sunitinib, sorafenib, or bevacizumab plus IFN. Patients who received prior 
immunotherapy (ie, received their targeted therapy as second-line 
treatment) also were included in the analysis. The analysis identified six 
clinical parameters to stratify patients into favorable, intermediate, and 
poor prognosis groups. Four of the five adverse prognostic factors are 
those previously identified by MSKCC as independent predictors of short 
survival: hemoglobin less than the LLN, serum-corrected calcium greater 
than the ULN, KPS <80%, and time from the initial diagnosis to initiation of 
therapy of <1 year. Additional, independent, adverse prognostic factors 
validated in this model are absolute neutrophil count (ANC) greater than 
the ULN and platelets greater than the ULN.114
Patients with none of the identified six adverse factors were in the 
favorable-risk category (n = 133; 22.7%) in which a median OS was not 
reached and a 2-year OS was 75% (95% CI, 65%–82%). Patients with 
one or two adverse factors were in the intermediate-risk category (n = 301; 
51.4%) in which a median OS was 27 months and a 2-year OS was 53% 
(95% CI, 46%–59%). Finally, those patients with three to six adverse 
factors were in the poor-risk category (n = 152; 25.9%) in which a median 
OS was 8.8 months and a 2-year OS was 7% (95% CI, 2%–16%).114 This 
model was validated in an independent dataset.116

header:
Treatment Options for Patients with Relapsed or Stage IV (M1 or 
Unresectable T4,M0) Disease
content:
Certain patients with stage IV disease also may benefit from surgery. For 
example, lymph nodes suspicious of metastatic disease on CT may be
hyperplastic and not involved with the tumor; thus, the presence of 
minimal regional adenopathy does not preclude surgery.
Cytoreductive nephrectomy before systemic therapy may be considered in 
select patients with stage IV, M1 disease and a potentially surgically 
resectable primary tumor mass. Generally, candidates for upfront 
cytoreductive nephrectomy have excellent performance status and no 
brain metastasis. A retrospective analysis conducted in the cytokine era 
indicated that patients most likely to benefit from cytoreductive 
nephrectomy before systemic therapy were those with lung-only 
metastases, good prognostic features, and good performance status.117 
Retrospective data from the IMDC suggested that cytoreductive 
nephrectomy continues to play a role in patients treated with VEGF-
targeted agents.118 The efficacy of newer systemic therapies is challenging 
the standard in some patients with metastatic disease. Results from the 
CARMENA phase III trial of patients with metastatic RCC who were 
eligible for cytoreductive nephrectomy found that sunitinib alone was non-
inferior to sunitinib after nephrectomy.119 The median OS was 18.4 months 
in the sunitinib-alone group and 13.9 months in the sunitinib after 
nephrectomy group (HR, 0.89; 95% CI, 0.71–1.10), which did not exceed 
the fixed non-inferiority limit (1.20). However, many of the patients in this 
trial had poor-risk features, underscoring the importance of patient 
selection to obtain the greatest benefit from nephrectomy or targeted 
therapy.119,120 A post-hoc analysis of the CARMENA trial reported that for 
patients with only one IMDC risk factor, OS was longer following 
nephrectomy (31.4 months vs. 25.2 months).121 At this point, there are no 
prospective data defining the role of cytoreductive nephrectomy in patients 
who subsequently receive checkpoint antibody therapy. However, the 
ongoing open-label, randomized, prospective SEVURO-CN trial is 
investigating initial cytoreductive nephrectomy prior to IO-based therapy, 
as well as deferred cytoreductive nephrectomy, compared to IO therapy 
only in patients with synchronous metastatic RCC (Clinical Trial ID:
PLEASE NOTE that use of this Oncology Guidelines Content is governed by the End-User License Agreement, and you MAY NOT distribute this Content or use it with any artificial intelligence model or tool.
Printed by Máximo López Chenlo on 5/4/2026 6:22:18 PM. Copyright © 2026 Oncology Guidelines, Inc. All Rights Reserved.
Version 2.2026 © 2026 Oncology Guidelines© (Oncology Guidelines©), All rights reserved. Oncology Guidelines® and this illustration may not be reproduced in any form without the express written permission of Oncology Guidelines.
Oncology Guidelines Version 2.2026 
Kidney Cancer
MS-13
NCT05753839). Further study will better define the role and timing of 
cytoreductive nephrectomy in the rapidly evolving treatment landscape for 
RCC. There are two ongoing randomized phase III trials investigating 
deferred cytoreductive nephrectomy following immuno-oncology (IO)-
based therapy in patients with synchronous metastatic RCC: the PROBE 
(SWOG 1931) trial (Clinical Trial ID: NCT04510597) and the NORDIC-
SUN trial (Clinical Trial ID: NCT03977571).
Besides cytoreductive nephrectomy, the Panel also recommends upfront 
systemic therapy for patients with stage IV, M1 potentially resectable 
primary disease and large-volume distant metastases or large 
sarcomatoid tumors over initial cytoreductive nephrectomy. For these 
patients, clinical trial is also an option (category 2B). In patients whose 
tumors are surgically unresectable (stage IV T4,M0 or M1), the Panel 
recommends performing tissue sampling to confirm diagnosis of RCC to 
determine histology and guide subsequent management. Systemic 
therapy is generally recommended after recurrence, after cytoreductive 
nephrectomy in patients with multiple metastatic sites, or for patients with 
surgically unresectable tumors. See Systemic Therapy Options for 
Patients with Relapsed or Stage IV Disease below for detailed 
recommendations.
Patients with metastatic disease who present with hematuria or other 
symptoms related to the primary tumor should be offered palliative 
nephrectomy if they are surgical candidates. In addition, the small subset 
of patients with potentially surgically resectable primary RCC and 
oligometastatic sites are candidates for nephrectomy and management of 
metastases by surgical metastasectomy; alternatively, SBRT or 
percutaneous ablation are available for selected patients as well. 
Candidates include patients who: 1) initially present with primary RCC and 
oligometastatic sites; or 2) develop oligometastases after a prolonged 
disease-free interval from nephrectomy. Oligometastatic sites that are
amenable to this approach include the lung, bone, and brain. The primary 
tumor and the metastases may be resected during the same operation or 
at different times. Most patients who undergo targeted treatment of 
oligometastases experience recurrence, but long-term relapse-free 
survival has been reported in these patients. Prospective phase II studies 
showed some patients may benefit from SBRT treatment for 
oligometastases, postponing time to systemic therapy.122-124 SBRT may 
offer durable local control for metastases.125 It should be considered for a 
patient with oligometastases unless metastasectomy is planned or SBRT 
cannot be delivered due to anatomic site, proximity to organs at risk, or 
previous treatment.122-127 For detailed recommendations on normal tissue 
constraints, as well as SBRT dosing and fractionation for distant 
metastatic disease, see Principles of Radiation Therapy in the Guidelines.
Patients who have undergone a nephrectomy and years later develop an 
oligometastatic recurrence also have the option of metastasectomy, 
SBRT,128-130 or percutaneous ablation, in addition to the first-line therapy 
options below. Clinical trial is an option for all patients, regardless of 
histology. Best supportive care is also recommended for all patients in this 
setting (see the Supportive Care section later in this Discussion).

header:
Systemic Therapy Options for Patients with Relapsed or Stage IV 
Disease
content:
Targeted therapy utilizing TKIs, and/or anti-VEGF antibodies, has been 
widely used in first- and second-line treatments. Agents targeting the 
mammalian target of rapamycin (mTOR) are also used in highly selected 
settings. A number of targeted agents have been approved by the FDA for 
the treatment of advanced RCC in the first and/or subsequent lines of 
therapy. ICIs provided a revolution in treatment options. Checkpoint 
antibodies alter the interaction between immune cells and antigen-
presenting cells, including tumor cells. These agents can augment an anti-
PLEASE NOTE that use of this Oncology Guidelines Content is governed by the End-User License Agreement, and you MAY NOT distribute this Content or use it with any artificial intelligence model or tool.
Printed by Máximo López Chenlo on 5/4/2026 6:22:18 PM. Copyright © 2026 Oncology Guidelines, Inc. All Rights Reserved.
Version 2.2026 © 2026 Oncology Guidelines© (Oncology Guidelines©), All rights reserved. Oncology Guidelines® and this illustration may not be reproduced in any form without the express written permission of Oncology Guidelines.
Oncology Guidelines Version 2.2026 
Kidney Cancer
MS-14
tumor immune response and have shown promise in a number of tumor 
indications.
Tumor histology and risk stratification of patients are important in therapy 
selection. The Oncology Guidelines for Kidney Cancer stratify treatment 
recommendations by histology. Recommendations for first-line treatment 
of ccRCC are also stratified by risk group based on the MSKCC 
Prognostic Model and the IMDC prognostic criteria.
Oncology Guidelines Categories of Preference 
To further guide management of advanced RCC, the Oncology Guidelines Kidney 
Cancer Panel has categorized all systemic kidney cancer therapy 
regimens as “Preferred,” “Other Recommended,” or “Useful in Certain 
Circumstances.” This categorization provides guidance on treatment 
selection by considering the efficacy, safety, evidence, and other factors 
that play a role in treatment selection. These factors include pre-existing 
comorbidities, nature of the disease, and in some cases consideration of 
access to agents.
Data Tables According to Line of Treatment and RCC Histology (Key 
Studies) 
Due to the increasing number of Oncology Guidelines-recommended systemic therapy 
options for metastatic RCC, the Panel has organized efficacy data from 
key studies into tables according to RCC histology and line of treatment 
(when applicable) for category 1 and 2A, preferred, and other 
recommended regimens; see Table 1, Table 2, and Table 3 at the end of 
this Discussion.
Information about drug mechanism of action, FDA approval, summaries of 
study conclusions and safety data, and Categories of Evidence and 
Consensus and Categories of Preference for Oncology Guidelines-recommended 
regimens remains below, and is stratified by RCC histology, line of

header:
treatment (when applicable), prior IO therapy status (when applicable), 
and Categories of Preference.
content:
First-Line Systemic Therapy Options for Patients with Stage IV (M1 
or Unresectable T4,M0) or Relapsed Clear Cell RCC
Preferred
Axitinib + Pembrolizumab (All Risk Groups) 
Axitinib is a selective, second-generation TKI of VEGFRs, while 
pembrolizumab is a monoclonal antibody that selectively binds to PD-1 
(expressed on activated T cells) and blocks the interaction between PD-1 
and its ligands PD-L1 and PD-L2 (both expressed on tumor cells and 
antigen-presenting cells). In April 2019, the FDA approved axitinib in 
combination with pembrolizumab for first-line treatment of patients with 
advanced RCC. Data from the randomized phase III KEYNOTE-426 trial, 
which included patients with favorable-, intermediate-, or poor-risk RCC, 
supported the combination therapy’s approval for this indication. Patients 
received either axitinib + pembrolizumab or sunitinib; those receiving the 
combination regimen had a significantly higher overall response rate 
(ORR) and longer PFS than those receiving sunitinib.131 Subsequent 
analyses at 31-, 43-, and 67-month median follow-ups showed agreement 
with these data (see Table 1 for most recent efficacy data).132-134 Median 
OS in the most recent follow-up (median 67.2 months) was longer in the 
axitinib + pembrolizumab arm compared to the sunitinib arm.134 Frequent 
AEs of grade 3 or higher in the axitinib + pembrolizumab arm included 
hypertension, diarrhea, and elevated alanine aminotransferase.132 Based 
on these data, the Panel recommends first-line axitinib + pembrolizumab 
as a category 1, preferred option for patients with ccRCC across all risk 
groups.
Cabozantinib + Nivolumab (All Risk Groups) 
Cabozantinib is a multitargeted TKI of VEGFRs, MET, and AXL, while 
nivolumab is an anti–PD-1 antibody. In January 2021, the FDA approved
PLEASE NOTE that use of this Oncology Guidelines Content is governed by the End-User License Agreement, and you MAY NOT distribute this Content or use it with any artificial intelligence model or tool.
Printed by Máximo López Chenlo on 5/4/2026 6:22:18 PM. Copyright © 2026 Oncology Guidelines, Inc. All Rights Reserved.
Version 2.2026 © 2026 Oncology Guidelines© (Oncology Guidelines©), All rights reserved. Oncology Guidelines® and this illustration may not be reproduced in any form without the express written permission of Oncology Guidelines.
Oncology Guidelines Version 2.2026 
Kidney Cancer
MS-15
cabozantinib in combination with nivolumab for first-line treatment of 
patients with advanced RCC. Data from the randomized phase III 
CheckMate 9ER trial, which included patients with favorable-, 
intermediate-, or poor-risk RCC, supported the combination therapy’s 
approval for this indication. Patients received either cabozantinib + 
nivolumab or sunitinib monotherapy. Those receiving cabozantinib + 
nivolumab had significantly higher ORRs and longer PFS than those 
receiving sunitinib. Additionally, OS was significantly longer with 
cabozantinib + nivolumab than sunitinib, and the HR favored cabozantinib 
+ nivolumab (HR for death, 0.60; 98.89% CI, 0.40–0.89; P = .001).135 In a 
subgroup analysis, the cabozantinib + nivolumab arm showed improved 
PFS, OS, and ORR in patients with advanced RCC with sarcomatoid 
features (an aggressive histologic subtype associated with poor prognosis) 
when compared to sunitinib.136 Patients receiving combination treatment 
also reported delayed time to deterioration of patient-reported outcome 
scores compared to sunitinib.137 Frequent AEs of grade 3 or 4 included 
hypertension, palmar-plantar erythrodysesthesia, and diarrhea.136 In the 
final analysis at median follow-up of 67.6 months, the cabozantinib + 
nivolumab combination continued to show clinically meaningful benefit 
over sunitinib, with longer OS and PFS and higher ORR (see Table 1 for 
recent efficacy data).138 Based on these data, the Panel recommends first-
line cabozantinib + nivolumab as a category 1, preferred option for 
patients with ccRCC across all risk groups.
Ipilimumab + Nivolumab (All Risk Groups) 
Ipilimumab is a monoclonal antibody that selectively blocks the interaction 
between the negative regulator cytotoxic T-lymphocyte antigen 4 (CTLA-4; 
expressed early on activated T cells) and its ligands CD80/CD86 
(expressed on antigen-presenting cells). Nivolumab’s mechanism of action 
was described previously. In April 2018, the FDA approved ipilimumab in 
combination with nivolumab for first-line treatment of patients with poor-
/intermediate-risk advanced RCC. Data from the randomized phase III
CheckMate 214 trial, which supported the FDA approval, compared 
combination ipilimumab + nivolumab followed by nivolumab monotherapy 
with sunitinib monotherapy in patients with advanced RCC.139 The study’s 
coprimary endpoints were ORR, OS, and PFS in patients with 
intermediate- and poor-risk RCC only; exploratory analyses of data in 
patients with favorable-risk RCC were reported separately. In patients with 
intermediate-/poor-risk RCC, combination ipilimumab + nivolumab led to a 
higher ORR and CR rate versus sunitinib monotherapy. Updated analyses 
continued to show a combined OS and ORR for intermediate-/poor-risk 
RCC that favored ipilimumab + nivolumab over sunitinib. In the primary 
analysis for patients with intermediate-/poor-risk RCC, longer OS was 
reported with ipilimumab + nivolumab versus sunitinib; however, PFS was 
not statistically significant per the predetermined threshold (HR for disease 
progression or death, 0.82; 99.1% CI, 0.64–1.05; P = 0.03).139 Longer 
follow-up suggested improved PFS, with an HR of 0.73 (95% CI, 0.62–
0.85).140,141
In initial analyses, OS benefit for patients with favorable-risk RCC with 
ipilimumab + nivolumab was not observed (HR, 1.45 at 25-month follow-
up);139 however, a trend towards improved OS in the combination was 
observed in an exploratory analysis of this patient population at the 8-year 
follow-up (HR, 0.82; 95% CI, 0.60–1.13).141 Updated efficacy data for all 
risk groups are found in Table 1. Treatment-related AEs occurred in 93% 
of patients in the ipilimumab + nivolumab group and 97% of patients in the 
sunitinib group; grade 3 or 4 events occurred in 46% and 63% of patients, 
respectively. AEs led to treatment discontinuation in 22% and 12% of 
patients receiving ipilimumab + nivolumab and sunitinib, respectively. 
Treatment-related deaths occurred in 8 patients receiving combination 
therapy and 4 patients receiving sunitinib. Thirty-five percent of patients 
who developed immune-mediated AEs after ipilimumab + nivolumab 
treatment received high-dose steroids.139 Based on these data, the Panel

Oncology Guidelines Version 2.2026 
Kidney Cancer
MS-16
recommends first-line ipilimumab + nivolumab as a category 1, preferred 
treatment option for patients with ccRCC across risk groups.
Lenvatinib + Pembrolizumab (All Risk Groups) 
Lenvatinib is a multitargeted TKI of VEGFR-1, -2, and -3; fibroblast growth 
factor receptor (FGFR)-1, -2, -3, and 4; platelet-derived growth factor 
receptor-α (PDGFR-α); c-KIT; and RET. Pembrolizumab’s mechanism of 
action was described previously. In August 2021, the FDA approved 
lenvatinib in combination with pembrolizumab for first-line treatment of 
patients with advanced RCC. Data from the randomized phase III CLEAR 
trial, which included patients with favorable-, intermediate-, or poor-risk 
RCC, supported the combination therapy’s approval for this indication. 
Patients received either lenvatinib + pembrolizumab, 
everolimus/lenvatinib, or sunitinib. Those receiving lenvatinib + 
pembrolizumab had significantly longer PFS and a higher ORR than those 
receiving sunitinib, which were maintained in follow-up analyses.142-144 At 
median 49-month follow-up, OS was favorable for lenvatinib + 
pembrolizumab compared to sunitinib (see Table 1 for most recent 
efficacy data).144 In contrast, OS was not significantly different between the 
everolimus/lenvatinib and sunitinib groups.142 The final analysis noted that 
~85% of patients treated with lenvatinib + pembrolizumab had treatment-
emergent AEs (grade 3 or higher), which included hypertension and 
diarrhea, versus ~75% of patients who received sunitinib, consistent with 
prior safety profile analyses.144 Based on these data, the Panel 
recommends first-line lenvatinib + pembrolizumab as a category 1, 
preferred treatment option for patients with ccRCC across all risk groups.
Cabozantinib (Poor-/Intermediate-Risk Groups) 
In the open-label, randomized, phase II CABOSUN trial, patients with 
intermediate- or poor-risk advanced RCC received either cabozantinib or 
sunitinib.145 See Table 1 for efficacy data. Those treated with 
cabozantinib showed a significantly increased PFS and higher ORR
compared to those treated with sunitinib. The percentage of patients who 
experienced AEs of any grade or grade 3 or 4 was similar between 
cabozantinib and sunitinib. The most frequently reported AEs with 
cabozantinib included diarrhea, hypertension, fatigue, and palmar-plantar 
erythrodysesthesia.145 Cabozantinib also increased quality-adjusted time 
without symptoms of disease or toxicity (Q-TWiST) versus sunitinib (317 
days vs. 180 days, respectfully, with no disease progression and no grade 
3 or 4 AEs).146
Based on these results, the Panel recommends first-line cabozantinib as a 
category 2A, preferred treatment option for patients with poor- and 
intermediate-risk ccRCC.
Other Recommended
Axitinib + Avelumab (All Risk Groups) 
Avelumab is a monoclonal antibody that selectively binds to PD-L1; 
axitinib’s mechanism of action was described previously. In May 2019, the 
FDA approved axitinib + avelumab for first-line treatment of patients with 
advanced RCC. Data from the randomized phase III JAVELIN Renal 101 
trial, which included patients with favorable-, intermediate-, or poor-risk 
RCC, supported the combination therapy’s approval for this 
indication.147,148 For both the overall population and PD-L1–positive 
patients, those receiving axitinib + avelumab had significantly longer PFS 
than those receiving sunitinib. This benefit was observed across all risk 
groups. For OS, data were immature for all groups in both the primary147 
and 13-month interim148 analyses. In the final analysis at an approximate 
median of 74 months, median OS was ~45 months with axitinib + 
avelumab versus ~39 months with sunitinib in the overall population but 
was not statistically significant (see Table 1).149 PFS was significantly 
longer and ORR was higher with the combination versus sunitinib.149,150 
Incidence of AEs of any grade, or grade 3 or higher, was similar between 
treatment arms,147 and no new safety signals were reported in the

Kidney Cancer
MS-17
extended follow-up.149,150 Based on these results, the Panel recommends 
first-line axitinib + avelumab as a category 2A, other recommended 
treatment option for patients with ccRCC across all risk groups.
The post-hoc analysis of 108 patients with sarcomatoid histology in the 
phase III JAVELIN Renal 101 trial showed that patients in the avelumab + 
axitinib treatment arm had improved PFS (stratified HR, 0.57; 95% CI, 
0.325–1.003) and a higher ORR (46.8% vs. 21.3%; complete response 
[CR] in 4.3% vs. 0%) versus those in the sunitinib arm.151
Pazopanib (All Risk Groups) 
Pazopanib is an oral multitargeted TKI/angiogenesis inhibitor of VEGFRs, 
PDGFR-α and -β, and c-KIT, ITK, LCK, and c-FMS. The drug’s safety and 
efficacy were evaluated in an open-label phase III study. Patients with 
advanced ccRCC who received 0–1 prior treatment received either 
pazopanib or placebo (see Table 1 for efficacy data). PFS was 
significantly longer and ORR was significantly higher with pazopanib 
versus placebo in the treatment-naïve sub-population,152 but there was no 
difference in OS between the two groups.153 Notable grade 3 toxicity was 
hepatotoxicity, indicated by elevated levels of alanine (30%) and aspartate 
(21%) transaminases.152 Therefore, it is critical to monitor liver function 
before and during treatment with the drug.
Additionally, the COMPARZ non-inferiority study of sunitinib versus 
pazopanib showed that these two drugs have similar safety and efficacy 
(see Table 1 for efficacy data).154,155 Based on these data, the Panel has 
listed first-line pazopanib as a category 2A, other recommended regimen 
for patients with ccRCC across all risk groups.
Sunitinib (All Risk Groups) 
Sunitinib is a multikinase inhibitor targeting several receptor tyrosine 
kinases, including PDGFR-α and -β; VEGFR-1, -2, and -3; c-KIT; FLT3; 
CSF-1R; and RET.156-159 The efficacy of first-line sunitinib was studied in a
randomized phase III trial in which patients with metastatic RCC received 
either sunitinib or IFN-α.156 See Table 1 for efficacy data. PFS was longer 
in those receiving sunitinib across all risk groups. Updated results 
demonstrated a strong trend towards OS advantage of sunitinib over IFN-
α in the first-line setting.160 Based on these data, the Panel includes first-
line sunitinib as a category 2A, other recommended regimen for patients 
with ccRCC across all risk groups.
Cabozantinib (Favorable-Risk Group) 
Extrapolating on the CABOSUN data for patients with poor-/intermediate-
risk (see above), the Panel added first-line cabozantinib as a category 2B, 
other recommended regimen for patients with favorable-risk ccRCC.
Useful in Certain Circumstances
Active Surveillance for Select, Asymptomatic Patients with ccRCC 
A subset of patients with advanced ccRCC show indolent progression of 
disease and could benefit from initial active surveillance because of the 
toxicity of systemic therapies. A phase II trial of patients with treatment-
naïve, asymptomatic, metastatic RCC followed patients on active 
surveillance through radiographic assessment at defined intervals until a 
decision was made to initiate systemic therapy.161 Of the 48 patients 
included in the analysis, the median time of surveillance from registration 
to initiation of systemic therapy was 14.9 months. This study demonstrated 
that a subset of patients with advanced ccRCC can safely undergo active 
surveillance before starting systemic therapy. In a prospective 
observational study of 504 patients with metastatic RCC, the median OS 
was not reached (95% CI, 122 months to not estimable [NE]) in patients 
who received active surveillance versus 30 months for those treated with 
systemic therapy.162,163 Therefore, the Panel included active surveillance 
as a category 2A, useful in certain circumstances option for select, 
asymptomatic patients with favorable-risk ccRCC.
PLEASE NOTE that use of this Oncology Guidelines Content is governed by the End-User License Agreement, and you MAY NOT distribute this Content or use it with any artificial intelligence model or tool.
Printed by Máximo López Chenlo on 5/4/2026 6:22:18 PM. Copyright © 2026 Oncology Guidelines, Inc. All Rights Reserved.

Oncology Guidelines Version 2.2026 
Kidney Cancer
MS-18
Axitinib (All Risk Groups)  
As a second-line therapy for patients with ccRCC, axitinib treatment led to 
higher ORR and longer median PFS compared with sorafenib.164 In a 
randomized phase III trial, treatment-naïve patients received either axitinib 
or sorafenib; PFS was not significantly longer in patients receiving axitinib 
versus sorafenib but had an acceptable toxicity profile.165 Based on these 
data, the Panel has included first-line axitinib as a category 2B, useful in 
certain circumstances option for patients with ccRCC across all risk 
groups.
Subsequent Systemic Therapy Options for Patients with Clear Cell 
RCC
The Oncology Guidelines Kidney Cancer Panel stratified the subsequent therapies for 
ccRCC based on whether the patients have received any prior IO therapy. 
The recommended options are categorized into “IO therapy naïve” and 
“prior IO therapy.” Certain regimens (ie, axitinib, cabozantinib, nivolumab, 
tivozanib) are category 2A options in this setting because of the Panel’s 
observation that randomized registrational trials for these monotherapies 
began prior to the approval of IO combination therapy, and very few 
patients enrolled on these trials received upfront IO combination therapy. 
Therefore, the data do not support category 1 level evidence for 
subsequent monotherapy after frontline TKIs in the era of IO combination 
therapy, despite the limited number of phase III trials for IO combinations 
in this setting.
Additionally, IO/TKI combinations for those who received prior IO therapy 
are designated as category 2B recommendations in this setting. Data to 
support the use of IO therapy following progression on IO therapy are 
lacking. Two randomized phase III trials investigating the use of IO/TKI 
combinations suggest IO therapy not be considered for patients who 
previously received it. In the CONTACT-03 trial, patients previously 
treated with IO with locally advanced or metastatic RCC were randomized
to either atezolizumab (PD-L1 inhibitor) + cabozantinib or cabozantinib 
monotherapy.166 PFS was not statistically significant between treatment 
arms (HR, 1.03; P = .78), and HR for death was 0.94 (P = .69). Serious 
AEs were reported for 48% of patients treated with the combination 
compared to 33% treated with cabozantinib. Discontinuation of treatment 
due to AEs occurred in 16% of patients with the combination, compared to 
4% with monotherapy.166 In the phase III TiNivo2 trial, patients with 
metastatic RCC who previously received IO therapy were randomized to 
either tivozanib + nivolumab or tivozanib alone.167 Likewise, PFS was 5.7 
months versus 7.4 months in the combination versus monotherapy arms, 
respectively (HR, 1.10; P = .49). The authors reported that this result was 
independent of where in the treatment sequence IO therapy was used.167
Cabozantinib  
In the randomized phase III METEOR trial, patients with disease 
progression after previous TKI therapy received cabozantinib or 
everolimus. See Table 2 for efficacy data. Median PFS was significantly 
longer and ORR significantly higher in patients receiving cabozantinib 
versus everolimus.168 The final analysis of the METEOR trial showed a 
statistically significant increase in OS in the cabozantinib arm versus the 
everolimus arm.169,170 Common grade 3 or 4 AEs that occurred more 
frequently with cabozantinib than with everolimus included hypertension, 
diarrhea, fatigue, hypomagnesaemia, and palmar-plantar 
erythrodysesthesia syndrome.168 Additionally, a network meta-analysis 
comparing the relative effectiveness of subsequent treatment options for 
advanced and metastatic ccRCC found cabozantinib offered greater PFS, 
OS, and ORR benefit over everolimus, lenvatinib monotherapy, 
nivolumab, pazopanib, axitinib, sorafenib, temsirolimus, and tivozanib, but 
not over everolimus/lenvatinib combination. The odds of severe AEs were 
improved with cabozantinib over everolimus/lenvatinib and lenvatinib 
monotherapy.171 Cabozantinib has also been used as the control arm for 
two studies that included patients who received prior IO therapy

Kidney Cancer
MS-19
(CONTACT-03, described above, and the randomized CANTATA trial). 
CANTATA was a randomized trial investigating the addition of glutaminase 
inhibitor telaglenastat to cabozantinib.172 In both studies, there was no 
statistically significant survival difference between the experimental and 
control arms.166,172
Based on these data, the Panel has included cabozantinib as a category 
2A, other recommended subsequent therapy option for patients with 
ccRCC regardless of their prior IO therapy status.
Everolimus/Lenvatinib 
In May 2016, the FDA approved lenvatinib, a multitargeted kinase 
inhibitor, in combination with everolimus, an mTOR inhibitor, for treating 
advanced RCC following one prior anti-angiogenic therapy. In a 
randomized phase II trial, patients with metastatic or unresectable, locally 
advanced ccRCC who had received prior antiangiogenic therapy received 
either combination everolimus/lenvatinib, single-agent lenvatinib, or single-
agent everolimus. See Table 2 for efficacy data. PFS and OS were 
significantly longer in patients receiving everolimus/lenvatinib versus 
everolimus monotherapy.173,174 Diarrhea was the most common grade 3 or 
4 AE for those receiving everolimus/lenvatinib.173 A prospective study of 
55 patients with metastatic ccRCC, heavily pretreated with prior ICIs and 
VEGFR-TKIs, showed a median PFS of 6.2 months and median OS of 
12.2 months with everolimus/lenvatinib.175 Based on the phase II trial data, 
the Panel considers everolimus/lenvatinib a category 2A, other 
recommended subsequent therapy option for patients with ccRCC 
regardless of their prior IO therapy status.
Nivolumab 
In the randomized phase III CheckMate 025 trial, patients with advanced 
ccRCC who were previously treated with one or more lines of anti-
angiogenic therapy (excluding mTOR inhibitors) received either nivolumab
or everolimus. See Table 2 for efficacy data. Patients receiving nivolumab 
had significantly longer OS and significantly higher ORR than those 
receiving everolimus.176 The most prevalent grade 3 or 4 AEs with 
nivolumab were fatigue, anemia, and elevated levels of alanine and 
aspartate aminotransferases.177 Based on these data, the Panel has 
included nivolumab as a category 2A, subsequent therapy option for 
patients with ccRCC who have not received any prior IO therapy.
Axitinib 
The randomized phase III AXIS study compared second-line axitinib 
versus sorafenib. See Table 2 for efficacy data. PFS was significantly 
longer and ORR significantly higher in patients receiving axitinib versus 
sorafenib.164 The most prevalent AEs of grade 3 or higher for those treated 
with axitinib included hypertension, diarrhea, and fatigue.178 Based on 
these data, the Panel included axitinib as a category 2A other 
recommended subsequent therapy option for patients with prior IO therapy 
and useful in certain circumstances for patients naïve for any prior IO 
therapy.
Axitinib + Pembrolizumab 
Upon axitinib + pembrolizumab’s FDA approval in a first-line setting, the 
Panel discussed whether the combination therapy might be used in clinical 
practice as an off-label subsequent treatment option in patients with 
relapsed or stage IV ccRCC. While they conceded that there were no 
robust, published data to support the use of axitinib + pembrolizumab in a 
second-line setting, they thought that clinicians were likely to consider the 
combination as a treatment option in patients with advanced ccRCC 
whose disease progressed after first-line sunitinib therapy. A retrospective 
study on 38 patients with ccRCC who had disease progression and 
previously received either ICI or VEGFR-TKI therapy were administered 
axitinib + pembrolizumab.179 Median PFS was 9.7 months (95% CI, 4.1–
15.3) at a median follow-up of 17.1 months. The ORR was 25% (all partial

Oncology Guidelines Version 2.2026 
Kidney Cancer
MS-20
responses [PRs]). Among 17 patients who had previously received 
ipilimumab + nivolumab and then second-line axitinib + pembrolizumab, 
the median PFS was 11.1 months and ORR was 31.4%. About 87% of 
patients in this study experienced AEs associated with the combination 
regimen, and hypertension, fatigue, and diarrhea were the most 
prevalent.179 The Panel added axitinib + pembrolizumab as a category 2A, 
other recommended option for patients who are IO therapy naïve. This 
combination is useful in certain circumstances for patients with prior IO 
therapy (category 2B).
Cabozantinib + Nivolumab 
Apolo et al 2020180 published data from an ongoing phase I dose 
escalation trial (ie, NCT02496208) in which patients with metastatic 
urothelial carcinoma or other genitourinary tumors (including three patients 
with ccRCC) received combination cabozantinib + nivolumab with or 
without ipilimumab; data from patients with ccRCC were not reported 
separately. A pooled analysis of the phase I dose-finding cohort and seven 
subsequent expansion cohorts, which included 16 patients with metastatic 
ccRCC, was reported.181 See Table 2 for efficacy data. In these patients, 
median OS was 43.6 months (95% CI, 19.4–NE). Although there are no 
prospective or retrospective published data showing the benefit of 
cabozantinib + nivolumab in later lines of therapy in the treatment of 
advanced RCC, the Panel’s decision was based on available data for 
combined ICI/TKI combinations of similar class such as lenvatinib + 
pembrolizumab and axitinib + pembrolizumab. The Panel added 
cabozantinib + nivolumab as a category 2A, other recommended option 
for patients who are IO therapy naïve. This combination is useful in certain 
circumstances for patients with prior IO therapy (category 2B).
Ipilimumab + Nivolumab 
The phase I CheckMate 016 trial included treatment-naïve patients and 
those who had received one to four or more prior treatment regimens.
Only the ORR results were stratified by treatment status: ORRs in the 
N3I1 and N1I3 were approximately 46% and 39%, respectively. OS and 
PFS data were not stratified by treatment line, but were similar.182 In a 
single-arm, phase II trial (TITAN-RCC), 32% of the 98 patients who had 
disease progression after second-line nivolumab had an objective 
response (OR) with ipilimumab/nivolumab. AEs of any grade occurred in 
83% of patients, with grade 3 or 4 AEs occurring in 40% of patients.183 In 
the randomized phase II FRACTION-RCC trial, patients whose disease 
progressed on or after IO therapy had an ORR of 17.4% on 
ipilimumab/nivolumab and median duration of response (DOR) of 16.4 
months (see Table 2). Similarly to TITAN-RCC, AEs of any grade occurred 
in 78% of patients, with AEs of grade 3 or 4 occurring in 28%.184 Two of 
the most common AEs of grade 3 or 4 reported in both trials were elevated 
lipase and diarrhea.183,184 Based on these data, the Panel considers 
ipilimumab + nivolumab as a category 2A, other recommended option for 
patients who are IO therapy naïve and useful in certain circumstances 
option for patients with prior IO therapy.
Belzutifan 
Belzutifan inhibits the transcription factor hypoxia-inducible factor 2α (HIF-
2α) and blocks the heterodimerization of HIF-2α with HIF-1β, thereby 
inducing tumor regression. Follow-up from an expansion cohort of 
patients, with ccRCC in a phase I/II trial of belzutifan, who had received 1 
or more prior therapies showed a disease control rate of 80% among 55 
patients. Median PFS was 14.5 months with 51% reporting PFS of 12 
months. The most common AEs reported were anemia, fatigue, hypoxia, 
and dyspnea, among others.185 Based on these results, belzutifan was 
considered well tolerated with a favorable safety profile as a single agent. 
Belzutifan was approved by the FDA for patients with advanced RCC who 
were previously treated with a PD-1 or PD-L1 inhibitor and VEGF-TKI. The 
open-label, randomized, phase III Litespark-005 trial compared belzutifan 
to everolimus in 746 patients with advanced ccRCC whose disease had

Kidney Cancer
MS-21
progressed after first-line therapies.186 At a median follow-up of 18.4 
months, median PFS and ORR for belzutifan were significantly favorable 
compared to everolimus (see Table 2). CR was observed in the interim 
analyses in the belzutifan arm but not in the everolimus arm. No 
statistically significant difference in OS was reported at a median follow-up 
of 25.7 months (see Table 2). The safety profile was similar to previous 
studies and incidence of grade 3–5 treatment-related AEs were similar 
between the two treatment arms.186
Based on these data, the Panel included belzutifan as a category 2A, 
other recommended regimen for patients who had prior IO therapy, 
particularly those who received PD-1/L1 and VEGF inhibitors. For patients 
who are IO therapy naïve, belzutifan is a category 2B, useful in certain 
circumstances subsequent therapy option for patients with ccRCC.
Lenvatinib + Pembrolizumab 
The phase II KEYNOTE-146 trial included three groups of patients: 
treatment-naïve; those who had previously received at least one line of 
treatment that did not include anti–PD-1 or anti–PD-L1 ICIs; and those 
who had previously received at least one anti–PD-1 or anti–PD-L1 ICI. 
See Table 2 for efficacy data. Treatment-naïve patients had the highest 
ORR and the longest PFS; ORR and PFS were comparable in the ICI-
naïve and ICI treatment-experienced groups. OS was longer only in the 
ICI-naïve group.187 In a longer follow-up, the ORR for treatment-naïve, 
previously treated but ICI-naïve, and ICI-pretreated populations were 
consistent with previous observations (see Table 2). The median DOR 
was 24.2 months, 9 months, and 14.1 months, respectively. The longer 
follow-up data showed OS and PFS benefit for lenvatinib + 
pembrolizumab in all three treatment groups with the treatment-naïve 
population showing the highest benefit, followed by ICI-pretreated groups, 
and then the pretreated ICI-naïve group.188 Hypertension, fatigue, and 
diarrhea were the most frequent treatment-related AEs and
hypothyroidism was the most frequent immune-related AE.187 Based on 
these data, the Panel considers lenvatinib + pembrolizumab a category 
2A, other recommended option for patients who are IO therapy naïve and 
a category 2B, useful in certain circumstances option for patients with prior 
IO therapy.
Tivozanib 
In March 2021, the FDA approved tivozanib, a multitargeted TKI of 
VEGFR-1, -2, and 3; c-KIT; and PDGFR-β for patients with relapsed or 
refractory advanced RCC who previously received two or more systemic 
therapies. Data from the randomized phase III TIVO-3 trial, which enrolled 
treatment-experienced patients with relapsed or refractory advanced 
ccRCC, supported the drug’s approval. See Table 2 for efficacy data. 
Patients receiving tivozanib had significantly longer PFS than those 
receiving sorafenib; OS was similar between the two groups.189,190 In the 
extended follow-up, a statistically meaningful, higher 12-month landmark, 
PFS-conditioned OS was observed in those treated with tivozanib versus 
sorafenib, suggesting that some patients may have an OS benefit with 
tivozanib.190,191 Tivozanib also increased Q-TWiST as compared to 
sorafenib (15.04 months vs. 12.78 months, respectively).192 Overall, 
incidence of AEs grade 3 or higher was lower in those treated with 
tivozanib compared to sorafenib. Common AEs grade 3 or higher were 
hypertension and asthenia.189,190 Additionally, tivozanib monotherapy was 
used as the control arm in the TiNivo-2 trial, as described previously, 
where there was no statistically significant PFS difference between the 
experimental and control arms in individuals who had received prior IO 
therapy.167
Based on these data, the Panel considers tivozanib as a category 2A, 
other recommended subsequent therapy option for patients who have 
received prior IO therapy and a useful in certain circumstances option for 
those who are IO therapy naïve.

Printed by Máximo López Chenlo on 5/4/2026 6:22:18 PM. Copyright © 2026 Oncology Guidelines, Inc. All Rights Reserved.

Kidney Cancer
MS-22
Lenvatinib 
The Panel also includes lenvatinib as a subsequent therapy option useful 
in certain circumstances (category 2A) regardless of prior IO therapy 
status based on the phase II trial investigating everolimus/lenvatinib, 
lenvatinib monotherapy, and everolimus monotherapy described 
above.173,174 PFS favored lenvatinib monotherapy over everolimus 
monotherapy (see Table 2 for efficacy data). There was no statistically 
significant difference in OS between lenvatinib and everolimus 
monotherapy. No significant difference in PFS or OS was observed 
between lenvatinib monotherapy and the everolimus/lenvatinib 
combination.173,174 Common grade 3 treatment-emergent AEs in those 
who received lenvatinib monotherapy included proteinuria, hypertension, 
and diarrhea. Hypothyroidism was reported more frequently in those 
receiving single-agent lenvatinib or the combination compared to 
everolimus.173
Pazopanib 
A phase III trial comparing pazopanib with placebo, detailed earlier under 
the Other Recommended Regimens for first-line ccRCC, also included 
patients who had received prior cytokine therapy. PFS was significantly 
longer with pazopanib (7.4 months) versus placebo (4.2 months) in the 
treatment-experienced sub-population (HR, 0.54; P < .001).152 However, 
OS was similar between the two groups (23 vs. 19 months, respectively; 
HR, 0.82).153 Additionally, a prospective phase II trial evaluated second-
line pazopanib in patients with advanced metastatic RCC previously 
treated with a targeted agent (ie, bevacizumab, sunitinib). Twenty-seven 
percent of patients had an OR to pazopanib; 49% had stable disease 
(SD). Median PFS was 7.5 months, regardless of prior treatment regimen. 
Estimated OS rate at 24 months was 43%. AEs were mostly grade 1 or 2, 
and common grade 3 and 4 AEs included diarrhea and hypertension, 
similar to the phase III study.193 Based on these data, the Panel considers 
pazopanib a category 2A, useful in certain circumstances subsequent
therapy option for patients with ccRCC regardless of their prior IO therapy 
status.
Sunitinib 
Sunitinib also has demonstrated substantial anti-tumor activity as a 
second-line therapy in patients with metastatic RCC who progressed on 
cytokine therapy.157,194 Sunitinib is considered a category 2A, useful in 
certain circumstances subsequent therapy option for patients with ccRCC 
regardless of their prior IO therapy status.
Everolimus 
Everolimus (RAD001) is an orally administered mTOR inhibitor. In the 
randomized phase III RECORD-1 trial, everolimus was compared with 
placebo for the treatment of metastatic RCC in patients whose disease 
had progressed on treatment with sunitinib or sorafenib. The median PFS 
was significantly longer for everolimus versus placebo, but OS was similar 
between the two groups.195,196 Common AEs included stomatitis, fatigue, 
and rash, which generally were not severe. Pneumonitis of any grade was 
detected in <15% of patients treated with everolimus.195,196 Everolimus is 
listed as a category 2A, useful in certain circumstances subsequent 
therapy option for patients with ccRCC regardless of their prior IO therapy 
status.
Bevacizumab 
Phase II trials have shown benefit of bevacizumab monotherapy after prior 
treatment with a cytokine. In a phase II study investigating low- and high-
dose bevacizumab versus placebo, bevacizumab extended time to 
progression of disease. Some patients receiving bevacizumab 
experienced hypertension or proteinuria, with no grade 4 or 5 AEs 
reported.197 Bevacizumab is a category 2B, useful in certain circumstances 
subsequent therapy option for patients with ccRCC regardless of their 
prior IO therapy status.

Kidney Cancer
MS-23

header:
Systemic Therapy for Patients with Stage IV (M1 or Unresectable 
T4,M0) or Relapsed Non-Clear Cell RCC
content:
Clinical trials of targeted agents have predominantly focused on patients 
with ccRCC due to the high prevalence of ccRCC.198 Data from systematic 
reviews, meta-analyses, and phase II studies with targeted agents also 
show some activity in patients with nccRCC. Compared with responses in 
ccRCC, however, the response rates with these agents are significantly 
lower for nccRCC. Therefore, according to the Panel, enrollment in clinical 
trials is a preferred strategy for nccRCC and tumor genomic testing should 
be considered for trial eligibility.
Non-Clear Cell RCC: Preferred
Cabozantinib 
The randomized phase II SWOG 1500 trial compared the MET-targeted 
TKIs cabozantinib, crizotinib, and savolitinib with standard-of-care sunitinib 
in patients with advanced papillary RCC who had previously received up 
to 1 previous systemic therapy, excluding VEGF- and MET-targeted TKIs. 
Assignment to the crizotinib and savolitinib arms was halted due to results 
of a prespecified futility analysis. See Table 3 for efficacy data. Patients 
receiving cabozantinib had significantly longer PFS and a higher ORR 
than those receiving sunitinib.199 Final OS analysis did not show any 
significant difference in survival.200 The incidence of AEs grade 3 or 4 was 
highest for cabozantinib (74%), and the most common grade 3 or 4 AEs 
for cabozantinib were hypertension, fatigue, and hand-foot syndrome.199 
Based on these data, the Panel included cabozantinib as a category 2A, 
preferred option for patients with nccRCC.
Cabozantinib + Nivolumab 
Two separate patient cohorts defined by nccRCC histology in a phase II 
open-label trial received cabozantinib + nivolumab combination.201 ORR 
for patients with papillary, unclassified, or translocation RCC was 48% with 
a median follow-up time of 34 months. Median PFS was 13 months (95%
CI, 7–16) and median OS was 28 months (95% CI, 23–43).202 The most 
prevalent AEs of any grade were fatigue, diarrhea, and palmar-plantar 
erythrodysesthesia syndrome, and the most common grade 3 or 4 event 
was hypertension.201 Study of patients with chromophobe RCC closed 
early due to the lack of efficacy. Based on these results, the Panel added 
cabozantinib + nivolumab as a category 2A, preferred option for first or 
subsequent-line treatment of stage IV (M1 or unresectable T4,M0) or 
relapsed nccRCC.
Lenvatinib + Pembrolizumab 
The phase II KEYNOTE-B61 trial investigated the combination of 
lenvatinib + pembrolizumab in untreated, advanced nccRCC.203,204 The 
primary endpoint was ORR, which was 49% in the initial analysis.203 A 
22.8-month follow-up showed similar results (51% ORR).204 See Table 3 
for all efficacy data. The most frequent treatment-related AEs of grade 3 or 
4 included hypertension, stomatitis, and proteinuria.203 The Panel included 
lenvatinib + pembrolizumab as a category 2A, preferred option for patients 
in this setting.
Non-Clear Cell RCC: Other Recommended
Erlotinib + Bevacizumab for Advanced Papillary RCC, Including 
Hereditary Leiomyomatosis and Renal Cell Carcinoma Associated RCC
Hereditary leiomyomatosis and renal cell carcinoma (HLRCC) is a 
hereditary condition in which affected patients are at risk for development 
of skin and uterine leiomyomas, as well as an aggressive form of papillary 
kidney cancer.205
In a phase II trial of patients with advanced papillary RCC (HLRCC-
associated RCC; n = 43 or sporadic papillary RCC; n = 40), patients were 
treated with bevacizumab plus erlotinib.206 Some patients who were 
enrolled with either subtype received previous therapy (21% with HLRCC; 
45% with sporadic papillary RCC), including a VEGFR TKI. The majority of

Printed by Máximo López Chenlo on 5/4/2026 6:22:18 PM. Copyright © 2026 Oncology Guidelines, Inc. All Rights Reserved.
